section.title: Absalom Returns
section.sectionRationale: Joab, sensing David's longing for his exiled son, sends a wise woman of Tekoa to move the king with a staged legal case, drawing from David a ruling that convicts him of keeping his own banished son away. David allows Absalom back to Jerusalem, but refuses to see his face for two years, a half-reconciliation that leaves the wound unhealed. Absalom, handsome and impatient, forces a meeting by burning Joab's field, and David finally kisses him. The movement is a study in incomplete reconciliation that will soon curdle into rebellion.
section.thesis: David is drawn to bring his banished son home, and the woman of Tekoa's parable even glimpses a God who devises ways for the banished not to remain cast out, yet David's reconciliation is half-hearted and incomplete, and the unresolved distance between father and son festers into the pride and ambition that will erupt in rebellion.
section.frameNote: Three beats: Joab and the wise woman of Tekoa move David to recall Absalom, verses 1 to 20; David permits Absalom's return but refuses to see him, verses 21 to 27; and Absalom forces a meeting and a cold kiss of reconciliation, verses 28 to 33.

units.label: The wise woman of Tekoa
units.note: God devises ways for the banished

verses.text: Now Joab son of Zeruiah perceived that the king’s heart longed for Absalom.

So Joab sent to Tekoa to bring a wise woman from there. He told her, “Please pretend to be a mourner; put on clothes for mourning and do not anoint yourself with oil. Act like a woman who has mourned for the dead a long time.
Then go to the king and speak these words to him.” And Joab put the words in her mouth.
When the woman from Tekoa went to the king, she fell facedown in homage and said, “Help me, O king!”
“What troubles you?” the king asked her. “Indeed,” she said, “I am a widow, for my husband is dead.
And your maidservant had two sons who were fighting in the field with no one to separate them, and one struck the other and killed him.

Now the whole clan has risen up against your maidservant and said, ‘Hand over the one who struck down his brother, that we may put him to death for the life of the brother whom he killed. Then we will cut off the heir as well!’ So they would extinguish my one remaining ember by not preserving my husband’s name or posterity on the earth.”
“Go home,” the king said to the woman, “and I will give orders on your behalf.”
But the woman of Tekoa said to the king, “My lord the king, may any blame be on me and on my father’s house, and may the king and his throne be guiltless.”
“If anyone speaks to you,” said the king, “bring him to me, and he will not trouble you again!”

“Please,” she replied, “may the king invoke the LORD your God to prevent the avenger of blood from increasing the devastation, so that my son may not be destroyed!” “As surely as the LORD lives,” he vowed, “not a hair of your son’s head will fall to the ground.”
Then the woman said, “Please, may your servant speak a word to my lord the king?” “Speak,” he replied.

The woman asked, “Why have you devised a thing like this against the people of God? When the king says this, does he not convict himself, since he has not brought back his own {{nadach|banished}} son?

For we will surely die and be like water poured out on the ground, which cannot be recovered. Yet God does not take away a life, but He {{chashab|devises}} ways that the banished one may not be cast out from Him.

Now therefore, I have come to present this matter to my lord the king because the people have made me afraid. Your servant thought, ‘I will speak to the king. Perhaps he will grant the request of his maidservant.
For the king will hear and deliver his maidservant from the hand of the man who would cut off both me and my son from God’s inheritance.’
And now your servant says, ‘May the word of my lord the king bring me rest, for my lord the king is able to discern good and evil, just like the angel of God. May the LORD your God be with you.’”
Then the king said to the woman, “I am going to ask you something; do not conceal it from me!” “Let my lord the king speak,” she replied.

So the king asked, “Is the hand of Joab behind all this?” The woman answered, “As surely as you live, my lord the king, no one can turn to the right or to the left from anything that my lord the king says. Yes, your servant Joab is the one who gave me orders; he told your maidservant exactly what to say.
Joab your servant has done this to bring about this change of affairs, but my lord has wisdom like the wisdom of the angel of God, to know everything that happens in the land.”

units.label: Absalom recalled but kept away
units.note: he must not see my face

verses.text: Then the king said to Joab, “I hereby grant this request. Go, bring back the young man Absalom.”
Joab fell facedown in homage and blessed the king. “Today,” said Joab, “your servant knows that he has found favor in your eyes, my lord the king, because the king has granted his request.”
So Joab got up, went to Geshur, and brought Absalom to Jerusalem.
But the king added, “He may return to his house, but he must not see my face.” So Absalom returned to his own house, but he did not see the face of the king.
Now there was not a man in all Israel as handsome and highly praised as Absalom. From the sole of his foot to the top of his head, he did not have a single flaw.
And when he cut the hair of his head, he shaved it every year because his hair got so heavy, he would weigh it out to be two hundred shekels, according to the royal standard.
Three sons were born to Absalom, and a daughter named Tamar, who was a beautiful woman.
Now Absalom lived in Jerusalem two years without seeing the face of the king.
Then he sent for Joab to send him to the king, but Joab refused to come to him. So Absalom sent a second time, but Joab still would not come.
Then Absalom said to his servants, “Look, Joab’s field is next to mine, and he has barley there. Go and set it on fire!” And Absalom’s servants set the field on fire.
Then Joab came to Absalom’s house and demanded, “Why did your servants set my field on fire?”

“Look,” said Absalom, “I sent for you and said, ‘Come here. I want to send you to the king to ask: Why have I come back from Geshur? It would be better for me if I were still there.’ So now, let me see the king’s face, and if there is iniquity in me, let him kill me.”
So Joab went and told the king, and David summoned Absalom, who came to him and bowed facedown before him. Then the king kissed Absalom.

nadach.gloss: To be banished, driven away, cast out, scattered. The woman speaks of David's banished son and of God's care for the banished one, the same word used for Israel driven into exile. It names the plight of the one shut out, and sets up the wonder that God, unlike David's half-hearted mercy, works so that the banished need not stay cast out from Him.

chashab.gloss: To devise, plan, reckon, purpose. The woman's striking theology is that God does not merely spare life but chashab, devises means, plans ways, so that the banished one may not remain cast out from Him. The word pictures God actively planning a way home for the exile, a plan that reaches its fullness in the gospel, where God devises the way for the cast out to be brought near through Christ.

basic.context: Absalom has been in exile at Geshur for three years after murdering Amnon, and David, though grieving, has not brought him home. Joab, seeing that the king longs for his son, arranges a scheme like Nathan's: he sends a wise woman from Tekoa to David disguised as a grieving widow. She presents a made-up legal case about one son who killed the other and a clan demanding the survivor's death, which would leave her without an heir. David rules to protect her surviving son. The woman then springs the point: in refusing to bring back his own banished son, the king convicts himself. She adds a beautiful line about God, who does not simply take life but devises ways so that the banished one need not remain cast out from Him. David recognizes Joab's hand behind it and agrees to recall Absalom, but with a crucial reservation: Absalom may live in Jerusalem, but must not see the king's face. So for two more years father and son live in the same city, estranged. The narrator notes Absalom's striking beauty and famous hair, hints of the vanity and pride growing in him. Frustrated, Absalom finally sets Joab's field on fire to force a meeting, and demands to see the king or be executed. David at last summons him and kisses him, but it is a cold, formal reconciliation. The chapter raises questions about half-hearted forgiveness, the difference between David's grudging mercy and God's, and how unhealed estrangement breeds rebellion.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative that must be read with discernment. The wise woman's words about God devising ways for the banished not to be cast out, verse 14, are true and beautiful, even though they come in the mouth of a woman coached by Joab for a manipulative purpose; the text lets a genuine glimpse of God's grace shine through a flawed scene. David's reconciliation with Absalom is presented as incomplete and unwise, a warning rather than a model. The chapter is descriptive: it shows how partial forgiveness and lingering estrangement create the conditions for the coming rebellion, without endorsing David's handling of it.
basic.originalLanguages: The woman speaks of the nadach, the banished one, driven away and cast out, David's own exiled son. Then comes the chapter's jewel: God does not merely spare life but chashab, devises means, actively plans ways, so that the banished one may not remain cast out from Him. The two words together set David's grudging, half-way mercy against God's inventive, pursuing grace, which contrives a way to bring the exile home, a grace fulfilled when God devises the way for the cast out to be brought near in Christ.
basic.typology: The woman's words in verse 14 offer a striking, if partial, glimpse of the gospel: a God who devises ways so that the banished one is not cast out from Him. This is exactly what God does in Christ, who Himself was cast out, crucified outside the city, so that the banished might be brought home. David's failure to reconcile fully with his banished son only sharpens the contrast with the Father who spares nothing to bring His exiled children all the way back.
basic.christ: The chapter's brightest line points beyond David to God, who devises a way that the banished not be cast out from Him. In Christ that way is fully devised: the Son bore banishment Himself, suffering outside the gate, so that exiles far from God might be welcomed all the way into the Father's presence, not merely permitted to live nearby with His face turned away. Where David reconciled with Absalom coldly and partially, keeping him at arm's length, the Father in Christ runs to embrace the returning prodigal and restores him fully.
basic.god: God is glimpsed in the woman's words as one who does not delight in taking life but plans ways for the banished to come home, a God of pursuing, inventive grace. This portrait of God stands in judgment over David's grudging, incomplete mercy, showing that human forgiveness so often falls short of the divine. God's grace is not half-way; He devises the whole way home for the cast out, revealing His heart to reconcile fully those estranged from Him.
basic.crossReferences: Second Samuel 12 recalls Nathan's staged parable that this scene deliberately echoes. Luke 15 tells of the father who runs to embrace his returning son, the full reconciliation David withholds. Hebrews 13 shows Christ suffering outside the gate as the banished one. And Ephesians 2 describes those once far off brought near by the blood of Christ.
basic.oneStory: Absalom is brought back from exile, and a wise woman glimpses the God who devises ways for the banished not to remain cast out, yet David's half-hearted reconciliation leaves the wound open and sets the stage for rebellion. The chapter's brightest truth outruns its flawed characters, pointing to the God who, in Christ, was Himself cast out so that His banished children might be brought all the way home.

context.literary: The chapter mirrors chapter 12: as Nathan used a parable to convict David of his sin, Joab uses the woman of Tekoa's staged case to convict him of neglecting his son, and David again passes judgment on himself. The narrator's note on Absalom's flawless looks and prized hair, verses 25 to 26, functions ironically, hinting at the vanity that fuels his ambition, and the hair foreshadows his death. The cold kiss of verse 33 caps a reconciliation that is anything but whole.
context.historical: The avenger of blood was a kinsman responsible to execute a murderer, and the woman's case exploits the tension between that duty and the need to preserve a family line. Tekoa was a town south of Bethlehem, later the home of the prophet Amos. Absalom's annual haircut, weighing several pounds, marks him as a figure of celebrated physical splendor. Setting fire to a neighbor's grainfield was a serious provocation, showing Absalom's willingness to use force to get his way, a preview of his rebellion.
context.canonical: The chapter advances the Absalom narrative from murder to return to the brink of revolt, and its theology of the banished being brought home resonates across Scripture, from Israel's exile and restoration to the New Testament's message of reconciliation. The woman's line in verse 14 has been treasured by the church as a window into God's redeeming heart, even as the surrounding story warns of the destruction bred by partial forgiveness and unresolved estrangement.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show how Absalom was brought back from exile through Joab's stratagem, to let a true glimpse of God's redeeming grace shine even through a manipulative scene, and to portray David's reconciliation as incomplete and unwise, so that the reader sees how unhealed estrangement prepares the way for the coming rebellion.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a court intrigue and a half-hearted reconciliation without commending David's handling; his refusal to see Absalom's face is presented as a failure, not a pattern to follow. What endures is the true theology voiced in verse 14, that God devises ways for the banished not to be cast out, and the warning that partial forgiveness and lingering distance breed resentment and disaster.

debates.question: How should we understand the wise woman's beautiful words about God in verse 14, given that she is manipulating David?

views.label: A true theological gem embedded in a flawed scheme
views.case: Though Joab coached her for a manipulative end, the words themselves, that God does not merely take life but devises means so the banished one may not be cast out from Him, express genuine biblical truth about God's redeeming grace; Scripture often lets true statements stand in imperfect mouths, and the church has rightly cherished this line as a window into God's heart, so its truth is not diminished by the scheme in which it appears.

views.label: Rhetoric aimed at David that nonetheless voices real grace
views.case: Others stress that the woman's primary aim is persuasive, to press David to act toward Absalom as she claims God acts, and that the theology serves her argument; even so, the argument works precisely because the underlying claim about God is true, so the verse still teaches a real principle even as it is deployed rhetorically.

debates.bearing: Both agree the statement about God is true and precious: God actively plans a way home for the banished; the debate only concerns how much weight to give its rhetorical setting, and either way the verse rightly points beyond David's grudging mercy to the fuller grace of God fulfilled in Christ, who was cast out that the banished might be brought near.

study.originalLanguages: The woman's speech turns on two Hebrew words. She speaks of the nadach, the banished one, the person driven away and cast out, the very word used elsewhere for Israel scattered into exile, and she applies it pointedly to David's own exiled son. Then comes the chapter's shining line: God does not merely spare life but chashab, devises means, actively plans and contrives ways, so that the banished one may not remain cast out from Him, verse 14. The verb pictures a God who does not passively wait for the exile but plots the route home for him. Set side by side, the two words expose the gap between David's mercy and God's: David will let his banished son return, but only to a house he may not leave to see the king's face, a grudging, half-way pardon, while God devises the whole way home for the cast out, a grace that finds its fullness when God contrives, at the cost of His own Son, to bring near those who were far off.

typology.type: God devising a way that the banished not be cast out
typology.fulfillment: Christ, cast out and crucified outside the gate, so that the banished might be brought home to God, Hebrews 13:12 and Ephesians 2:13.
Contrasted with the Father who runs to fully embrace the returning prodigal, Luke 15:20-24.

typology.type: The handsome son whose glory feeds his pride
typology.fulfillment: Contrasted with Christ, who had no outward majesty yet humbled Himself, Isaiah 53:2 and Philippians 2:8.

study.christ: The chapter's brightest line points beyond David to God, who devises a way that the banished not be cast out from Him, and that way is fully devised in Christ. The Son of God became the banished one, suffering outside the city gate, Hebrews 13:12, so that exiles far from God might be brought near, not merely permitted to live in the same city with the King's face turned away, but welcomed all the way into the Father's presence, Ephesians 2:13. Where David reconciled with his banished son coldly and partially, keeping him at arm's length for years, the Father in Christ runs to embrace the returning prodigal, restoring him fully as a son, Luke 15:20-24. David's failure only sharpens the wonder of the God who spares nothing to bring His exiled children all the way home.
study.god: God is glimpsed in the woman's words as one who does not delight in taking life but plans ways for the banished to come home, a God of pursuing, inventive grace who contrives the route back for the exile. This portrait stands in quiet judgment over David's grudging, incomplete mercy, exposing how far human forgiveness so often falls short of the divine. God's grace is never half-way: He devises the whole way home for the cast out, welcoming them fully rather than keeping them at a distance. The verse reveals the very heart of God to reconcile completely those who are estranged from Him, a heart poured out at last in the sending of His Son.

crossReferences.note: Nathan's staged parable that convicted David of his sin, deliberately echoed by the woman of Tekoa's staged case.
The father who runs to embrace and fully restore his returning son, the complete reconciliation David withholds from Absalom.
Jesus suffered outside the gate as the banished one, that His people might be brought near and made holy.
Those who were once far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ, the way home God devises for the banished.

study.oneStory: Absalom is brought back from exile, and a wise woman voices a glimpse of the God who devises ways so that the banished one need not remain cast out from Him, yet David's half-hearted reconciliation, keeping his son near but unseen, leaves the wound open and prepares the ground for rebellion. The chapter's brightest truth outruns its flawed and scheming characters, pointing beyond David's grudging mercy to the God who, in Christ, was Himself cast out that His banished children might be welcomed all the way home. What David could only do coldly and partially, God does fully and joyfully in the gospel.

quiz.q: How does Joab move David to bring Absalom back?

options.text: He sends a wise woman of Tekoa with a staged legal case that leads David to convict himself for keeping his banished son away.
options.note: Verses 1-20 show Joab's stratagem echoing Nathan's parable to move David.

options.text: He openly rebukes David for failing to forgive his son.
options.note: Joab works indirectly through the woman's parable, not by open rebuke.

options.text: He uses a disguised woman to press David toward reconciliation with Absalom.
options.note: True, but this option omits how the staged case leads David to pronounce judgment that convicts himself.

options.text: He threatens to resign unless David recalls Absalom.
options.note: Joab makes no such threat; he arranges the wise woman's appeal.

quiz.q: What beautiful truth about God does the woman of Tekoa voice?

options.text: That God does not merely take life but devises ways so that the banished one may not remain cast out from Him.
options.note: Verse 14 is the chapter's theological jewel about God's redeeming grace toward the banished.

options.text: That God always spares the guilty from any earthly consequence.
options.note: The verse speaks of God devising ways home for the banished, not sparing all consequences.

options.text: That God plans a way to bring back those who are cast out.
options.note: True, but this option loses the striking contrast that God does this rather than simply taking life away.

options.text: That God helps those who first make themselves worthy of return.
options.note: The verse stresses God's own initiative in devising the way home, not the exile earning it.

quiz.q: What condition does David place on Absalom's return?

options.text: Absalom may live in Jerusalem but must not see the king's face, a partial, incomplete reconciliation.
options.note: Verse 24 records David's reservation that keeps Absalom at a distance for two years.

options.text: Absalom is fully restored to his place as a son at once.
options.note: The opposite; David refuses to see Absalom's face, leaving the reconciliation incomplete.

options.text: Absalom may return home but is kept from the king's presence.
options.note: True, but this option omits how long the estrangement lasts and its role in breeding Absalom's resentment.

options.text: Absalom must first make restitution for Amnon's death.
options.note: No restitution is required; the condition is simply that he not see the king's face.

quiz.q: How does Absalom finally force a meeting with the king?

options.text: He sets Joab's barley field on fire to make Joab arrange a meeting, then demands to see the king or be killed.
options.note: Verses 28-32 show Absalom's forceful, impatient maneuver to reach the king.

options.text: He waits patiently until David is ready to summon him.
options.note: The opposite; Absalom acts forcefully, burning Joab's field to compel a meeting.

options.text: He pressures Joab into arranging an audience with the king.
options.note: True, but this option omits the striking, aggressive act of burning Joab's field that reveals Absalom's character.

options.text: He publicly appeals to the people to demand his restoration.
options.note: That public appeal comes in the next chapter; here he burns Joab's field to force the meeting.

quiz.q: How does David's reconciliation with Absalom contrast with the grace of God?

options.text: David's is cold and partial, keeping his son at a distance, while God devises the whole way home, fully welcoming the banished in Christ.
options.note: The contrast points to the Father who fully restores, Luke 15:20-24 and Ephesians 2:13.

options.text: David's reconciliation shows that God only forgives after a long probation.
options.note: God's grace runs to embrace fully; David's grudging delay is the failure the text exposes, not God's pattern.

options.text: David reconciles incompletely, unlike the God who brings the banished all the way home.
options.note: True, but this option omits the gospel depth that God does this through Christ, the banished one cast out for us.

options.text: David's kiss proves that human forgiveness is always as full as God's.
options.note: The cold kiss shows the opposite; human forgiveness here falls far short of God's complete welcome.

questions: David brought Absalom back but would not see his face, a forgiveness that was really only half done. Is there someone you have 'forgiven' in word while still keeping them at arm's length?
The woman of Tekoa spoke of a God who devises ways for the banished not to be cast out. Where do you need to trust that God is actively planning a way home for you or someone you love?
David's incomplete reconciliation left a wound that festered into rebellion. How might unresolved estrangement in your relationships be quietly breeding resentment?
Absalom's flawless looks and famous hair hint at a growing vanity and pride. Where might outward gifts or admiration be feeding pride in your own heart?
In Christ, God did not keep His returning children at a distance but welcomed them fully, at the cost of His Son being cast out. How does that full welcome free you to forgive others completely?

translationNotes.note: The woman's words, that God 'devises ways that the banished one may not be cast out from Him,' voice a genuine truth about God's redeeming grace, even though spoken as part of Joab's staged appeal; the church has long treasured this line as a window into God's heart, fulfilled in Christ.

The BSB footnote notes that Absalom's annual haircut weighed about two hundred shekels, roughly five pounds of hair; the detail marks his celebrated splendor and darkly foreshadows his death, when his hair catches in a tree, chapter 18.
The BSB footnote notes that the Septuagint adds Joab's servants reporting the burning of his field with torn clothes; the BSB main text follows the shorter Hebrew reading.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Samuel 14:1-33. An em dash in the source at verse 26 was converted to a clean break with commas; no dashes remain in this file.
